Q1: I had a pretty structured routine, honestly. I'd block out specific times during the week—mostly mornings and early afternoons—to work through my eleven courses. I relied heavily on listening to lecture recordings while I did other things, which worked well for my auditory learning style. I kept a planner and tried to tick off tasks systematically, even if I wasn't always physically in class. The routine kept me sane, especially with the high stress I was dealing with.
Q2: I tried really hard to keep regular times, but with eleven online courses, deadlines would sometimes pile up and throw everything off. When that happened, I'd shift into crisis mode and just focus on whatever was due soonest. But generally, I preferred consistency—it helped me feel less scared about falling behind. I'd say about 70% of the time I stuck to my schedule, and the other 30% was reactive.
Q3: Usually it was to check if there were new announcements or upcoming deadlines. I logged in frequently—probably out of habit and a bit of anxiety about missing something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch lecture videos or catch up on discussion posts. The fear of being caught off guard really motivated me to stay connected to the platform.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became automatic, like checking my email. I think it happened because I set reminders on my phone and made it part of my morning routine. Once I associated it with my coffee and breakfast, it just stuck. It became a comfort thing too—checking in made me feel like I had some control over my courses.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times to really absorb the content. The discussion boards were also helpful because reading other students' perspectives reinforced what I'd heard. I also appreciated when instructors posted audio summaries or podcasts—those felt tailored to how I learn.
Q6: I tended to skim over the supplementary reading PDFs and some of the external links. They felt overwhelming when I was already juggling eleven courses, and reading just isn't how I absorb information best. I knew they were there, but I'd only open them if I was really struggling with a concept. It wasn't that they weren't valuable—I just had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it helped clarify something I was confused about. I also valued anything I could listen to while multitasking. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my grade, I'd deprioritize it. Time is precious when you're managing this many courses.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With required work taking up so much of my 27 hours of study time, optional stuff got pushed aside unless I was genuinely curious. Required activities had urgency attached to them, while optional ones felt like 'nice to have' rather than necessary. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping them, but I had to be realistic.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and lecture recordings to my phone so I could listen while commuting or doing chores. I also took handwritten notes during lectures and reviewed those offline. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen, just processing what I'd heard. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that.
Q10: There were many times I'd listen to a downloaded lecture two or three times while walking or cooking, really engaging with the material, but the LMS would only show that I accessed it once. Or I'd discuss concepts with a friend over the phone to test my understanding. That kind of active learning doesn't leave a digital footprint, but it was crucial for me.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download the audio or video once, then study it offline for days. I'd pause, rewind, take notes—all without being connected to the LMS. It was more convenient and saved my data. The system would just show a single access, but the reality was much more intensive engagement.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear crept in and made it hard to focus. When I felt overwhelmed by the sheer volume of work across all my courses, I'd freeze up a bit. The CBT sessions I attended helped me work through some of that, but there were still days when I just felt scared about whether I was doing enough. Those were the hardest times to engage.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. Twenty-seven hours of studying per week across eleven courses is a lot, and sometimes I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen. Competing responsibilities—like part-time work and family stuff—also affected my focus. I'd access materials but not really process them because my brain was elsewhere.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too stressed or tired. I'd have to go back and rewatch it later. The LMS would show I completed it, but the first viewing was basically meaningless. It's frustrating when your body is there but your mind isn't.
Q15: I postponed some discussion posts even though I knew participation mattered. Writing out my thoughts felt more draining than listening, so I'd put it off. Sometimes fear of saying something wrong held me back too. I'd eventually do them, but often at the last minute, which wasn't ideal for quality.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver, honestly. I'd organize my whole week around what was due when. The LMS calendar was always open, and I'd work backward from deadlines to plan my study sessions. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged as consistently—they gave structure to my otherwise overwhelming course load.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me. When I got encouraging comments, I felt more motivated to engage with similar activities. When feedback was critical, I'd spend extra time reviewing related materials to improve. The CBT sessions also taught me to reframe negative feedback as learning opportunities rather than personal failures, which helped a lot.
Q18: The course structure mattered a lot. Courses with clear weekly modules and predictable patterns were easier for me to manage. When expectations were laid out upfront, I could plan my routine around them. Courses that were disorganized or had surprise assignments really threw me off and increased my stress.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. Clear navigation and well-labeled modules helped too. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on dense reading or had confusing assignment instructions. Accessibility of different content formats really affected my ability to engage.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the LMS without actually completing work—like a nervous habit. Or maybe they're struggling with the material and keep reviewing it but can't translate that into finished assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when stress is involved.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was low—about 20%—but I still passed because I engaged heavily with recorded materials offline. Someone could watch everything once, study it deeply on their own, and perform well without the LMS showing much activity. The platform doesn't capture offline learning at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you log in, what you click on, how often you participate in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture emotional states or whether someone was actually focused during their logged time.
Q23: Being engaged means actively trying to understand and apply the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about listening carefully, asking myself questions, participating in discussions meaningfully, and connecting concepts to real life. It's a mental investment, not just completing tasks. True engagement feels purposeful.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on audio learning away from the platform, or the mental effort I put into managing my fear and stress. It wouldn't capture the CBT strategies I used to stay resilient, or the conversations I had with peers outside the system. My engagement was much deeper than what any log could reflect.
